critique.summary: The article reports that the FBI has resumed purchasing Americans' location data from commercial data brokers to aid federal investigations, as confirmed by FBI Director Kash Patel during testimony to lawmakers. This practice raises constitutional concerns, particularly regarding the Fourth Amendment, as it allows the FBI to obtain location data without a warrant by buying it from third-party companies.
mainPoints: FBI Director Kash Patel confirmed the agency is buying commercially available location data from data brokers.
This is the first confirmation since 2023 that the FBI is actively purchasing such data.
The data is sourced from consumer phone apps and games, collected by data brokers.
Senator Ron Wyden criticized the practice as circumventing the Fourth Amendment's warrant requirements.
The FBI claims it does not need a warrant to use commercially purchased data, though this legal theory has not been tested in court.
Lawmakers have introduced the Government Surveillance Reform Act to require warrants before federal agencies can buy Americans' data from brokers.
critique.truthfulness: The article's claims are consistent with publicly available information and recent congressional discussions. FBI Director Kash Patel's testimony and Senator Wyden's statements are documented in credible news sources such as Politico. The practice of federal agencies purchasing location data from brokers to circumvent warrant requirements has been reported by multiple outlets and is a subject of ongoing legal and legislative debate. The article accurately reflects these facts and presents multiple perspectives, including the FBI's justification and the constitutional concerns raised by lawmakers.
considerations: The article does not detail how frequently or extensively the FBI uses purchased location data in investigations.
It does not provide specific information about which data brokers supply the FBI or the exact nature of the data purchased.
The legal status of using commercially purchased data without a warrant remains unsettled and could be challenged in courts.
The article does not explore the privacy implications for individuals beyond the constitutional debate.
There is no discussion of potential safeguards or oversight mechanisms governing the FBI's use of purchased data.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers on the FBI's purchase of location data and the broader implications of data privacy, surveillance, and legal frameworks. Commenters highlight the complex supply chain of location data, call for stronger legislation, and express concerns about government overreach and private sector complicity.

comments.author: FL4TLiN3
comments.text:
Who's selling the data is the far more serious issue here. Behind this is a remarkably well-structured syndicate.
The supply chain looks something like this: consumer apps embed ad SDKs → those SDKs feed location signals into RTB ad exchanges → surveillance-oriented firms sit in the RTB pipeline and harvest bid request data even without winning auctions → that data flows to aggregators who don't have any direct relationship with consumers → and from there it's sold to government agencies, among others.
The genius of this structure is that accountability dissolves at every layer. Each intermediary can claim they're just passing along "commercially available data." Nobody verifies whether consumers actually consented to their location data being collected and resold. The consent verification is always someone else's job.
The real problem is that this data is buyable at all, by anyone, through an opaque multi-layered supply chain specifically designed so that no single entity bears responsibility for the end result.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a detailed and plausible explanation of how location data is collected and sold, highlighting the complexity and lack of accountability in the supply chain.

comments.author: lukeschlather
comments.text:
I'd really like to just have legislation to treat location data like audio or video under wiretapping provisions. If you collect my location info and convey it to a third party without my consent or a reasonable good-faith belief that I would consent, that ought to be treated similarly to recording without consent.

And consent needs to be granted explicitly for each party that might get access to my location, you can't just get blanket consent to sell my location to anyone, especially not with real-time identifiable location data.
comments.analysis: This comment thoughtfully proposes a legal framework to protect location data, emphasizing explicit consent and likening location tracking to wiretapping, which is a relevant and constructive suggestion.

comments.author: nomel
comments.text:
The supreme court had a 5-4 decision related to this [1]. Was there something specific, in that decision, that leaves a loophole open?

[1] https://www.supremecourt.gov/opinions/17pdf/16-402_h315.pdf
comments.analysis: This comment references a relevant Supreme Court decision, prompting discussion about legal precedents and potential loopholes, adding important context to the debate.

comments.author: josefritzishere
comments.text: A generation ago our leaders derided China (and Russia) for this kind of pervasive spying on it's citizens. In the US we did the same thing just increasing costs by enriching the private sector on the way. That's not better. That's worse.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a historical and comparative perspective on surveillance, noting the US government's reliance on private sector intermediaries, which is a valid critique.

comments.author: rootusrootus
comments.text: Perhaps we could overturn the third party doctrine.  With legislation, preferably.  And while we are at it, solve the underlying issue of pervasive data collection and sharing in the first place.
comments.analysis: This comment identifies a key legal doctrine relevant to data privacy and suggests legislative reform, contributing constructively to the discussion.

comments.author: givemeethekeys
comments.text: That's the job of the FBI - to investigate domestic crimes. But, why do private organizations so willingly participate in the tracking ecosystem? I suppose they're in the, "you have nothing to worry about if you're not doing anything illegal" camp! Hopefully they understand that they have the most to lose.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration with private companies' participation in surveillance, reflecting a common sentiment though it lacks deeper analysis.

comments.author: nullcathedral
comments.text: Yikes. Why are private organizations so happy to participate in mass surveillance.
comments.analysis: A brief emotional reaction questioning private sector involvement, accurate in sentiment but lacking detail or constructive input.

comments.author: jimt1234
comments.text: This should be a surprise to absolutely no one. I think it sucks, but I also don't think it's anything new.
comments.analysis: This comment expresses resignation about the news, correctly noting that such surveillance practices have been ongoing.

comments.author: SilverElfin
comments.text: The government shouldn’t be able to contract out anything it isn’t permitted to do directly itself. We should have this in the law, get rid of qualified immunity for everyone including lawmakers, and reign in the government.
comments.analysis: This comment advocates for legal reforms to limit government overreach and increase accountability, a relevant and constructive viewpoint.

comments.author: shevy-java
comments.text:
They hate us for our freedom.

Also, isn't this breaking the constitution? It bypasses needing a warrant respectively having a objective suspicion.
comments.analysis: This comment emotionally asserts constitutional concerns about warrantless surveillance, a valid point though it lacks legal nuance.

comments.author: thenthenthen
comments.text: There was a great talk at the Chaos Computer Conference a few years ago how to diy this, sadly cant find it because web search seems dead nowaydays. If anyone knows, please chip in. It was a german researcher following german politicians who hilariously(scandalously?) related travel patterns
comments.analysis: This comment is anecdotal and does not contribute substantive information or analysis to the discussion.

comments.author: rasz
comments.text: To be fair they are only doing that in order to track if his honeypot brib^de isnt cheating on him.
comments.analysis: This comment appears to be trolling or sarcastic and does not add meaningful content to the discussion.

comments.author: themafia
comments.text:
I have to give my age to my OS.

Yet they can't write a law to make this basic practice illegal.

Why do I feel like I'm not being represented _at all_?
comments.analysis: This comment expresses frustration about lack of legal protections but offers no detailed insight or constructive suggestions.
